before: Lush Life is a studio album by the jazz musician John Coltrane. It was released in early 1961 by Prestige Records. It was assembled from previously unissued tracks from three recording sessions at Van Gelder Studio in Hackensack, New Jersey in 1957 and 1958. As Coltrane's profile increased during the 1960s, some years after the end of his Prestige contract, the label used unissued recordings to create new albums without Coltrane's input or approval.
after: Soul Junction is an album led by jazz pianist Red Garland, released in 1960 on Prestige. It draws on a November 15, 1957 session with John Coltrane on tenor saxophone and Donald Byrd on trumpet, the same lineup that cut All Mornin' Long. The program runs from the long title blues to Dizzy Gillespie's "Woody 'n You" and "Birks' Works" and Duke Ellington's "I've Got It Bad."
evidenceUsed: wiki extract (Red Garland, 1960 Prestige, Nov 15 1957 session, same lineup as All Mornin' Long, personnel Coltrane/Byrd) + MB tracklist for track titles

before: Holiday Soul is an album of Christmas music by the American jazz pianist Bobby Timmons, recorded in 1964 and released on the Prestige label. Captures pianist Bobby Timmons during the cool jazz movement, favoring lighter timbres, relaxed tempos, and a chamber sensibility.
after: Soul Time is an album by jazz pianist Bobby Timmons, released on Riverside in November 1960. The set mixes Timmons originals such as the title track and "Stella B." with standards including "The Touch of Your Lips" and "S'posin'."
evidenceUsed: MB release (Bobby Timmons, Riverside RS 9334, 1960-11) + tracklist for track titles

before: Ballads is a solo studio album by English guitarist Derek Bailey. It was released on 23 April 2002, through Tzadik. In 2007, The Guardian included the album in their list of "1000 Albums to Hear Before You Die".
after: Standards is an album by English guitarist Derek Bailey, released on Tzadik in 2007. Tracks carry Bailey's wry titles such as "Nothing New," "Frankly My Dear I Don't Give a Damn," and "When Your Liver Has Gone."
evidenceUsed: MB release (Derek Bailey, Tzadik TZ 7620, 2007-06-26) + tracklist for track titles

before: Sail Away is an album by the American musician Tom Harrell. It was released in 1989 via Contemporary Records. The title track became a jazz standard. The album was reissued in 2003 with two bonus tracks.
after: Stories is an album by American trumpeter Tom Harrell, released in 1988. It collects Harrell originals including "Rapture," "Songflower," "The Mountain," and "Story."
evidenceUsed: MB release (Tom Harrell, 1988, no label) + tracklist for track titles

before: Modern Art is an album by saxophonist Art Pepper featuring sessions recorded in late 1956 and early 1957 originally released on the Intro label. The album was reissued on CD on Blue Note Records with bonus tracks as Modern Art: The Complete Art Pepper Aladdin Recordings Volume 2 in 1988. Active primarily in West Coast jazz, Pepper first came to prominence in Stan Kenton's big band.
after: Straight Life - The Savoy Sessions is a collection of recordings by saxophonist Art Pepper, issued on Savoy Jazz in 1984. The program includes the title track along with "Chili Pepper," "Cinnamon," "Tickle Toe," and "Suzy the Poodle."
evidenceUsed: MB release (Art Pepper, Savoy Jazz ZD70820, 1984) + tracklist for track titles

before: Night Lights is an album by American jazz saxophonist Gerry Mulligan featuring performances recorded in 1963 and first released on the Philips label. The album's re-release on the Verve Label, includes the bonus track "The Lonely Night " which originally only appeared on Mulligan's 1965 album "Feelin' Good" with vocals by his partner, Judy Holliday.
after: Summit (Reunión cumbre) is a 1974 collaboration between saxophonist Gerry Mulligan and bandoneonist Astor Piazzolla, released on Erre T.V. The album pairs the two leaders on pieces such as "20 Years Ago," "Years of Solitude," "Close Your Eyes and Listen," and "20 Years After."
evidenceUsed: MB release (Gerry Mulligan & Astor Piazzolla, Erre T.V. RTV 25046, 1974) + tracklist for track titles

before: Pianist Art Tatum recorded commercially from 1932 until near his death. He recorded nearly 400 titles, if airchecks and informal, private recordings are included. He recorded for Brunswick (1933), Decca (1934–41), Capitol and for the labels associated with Norman Granz (1953–56).
after: Tatum, Carter, Bellson is a 1954 album on Clef Records pairing pianist Art Tatum with Carter and Bellson. The session works through standards including "My Blue Heaven," "Street of Dreams," "Idaho," and "'s Wonderful," alongside "Blues in B Flat."
evidenceUsed: MB release (Tatum/Carter/Bellson, Clef MG C-643, 1954) + tracklist for track titles

before: That Certain Feeling is an album by American organist John Patton recorded in 1968 and released on the Blue Note label. A contemporary jazz entry from 1991 by Ran Blake, documenting the breadth of the contemporary period.
after: That Certain Feeling is a 1991 album by pianist Ran Blake. It is built around George Gershwin material, with tracks such as "It Ain't Necessarily So," "The Man I Love," "Mine," and "Oh Where's My Bess?"
evidenceUsed: MB release (Ran Blake, 1991) + tracklist (Gershwin song titles) for track names

before: Jean Reinhardt, known by his Romani nickname Django, was a Belgian-born Romani jazz guitarist and composer who lived most of his life in France. He was one of the first major jazz talents to emerge in Europe and has been hailed as one of its most significant exponents.
after: The Great Artistry of Django Reinhardt is a 1953 album collecting recordings by guitarist Django Reinhardt. It gathers performances including "Nuages," "Insensiblement," "Night and Day," "Brazil," and "Blues for Ike."
evidenceUsed: MB releaseGroup (The Great Artistry of Django Reinhardt, firstReleaseDate 1953-11) + tracklist; year/label kept from ours

before: A Handful of Keys is an album of live performances by jazz pianist Fats Waller & His Rhythm, the only authorized live performances of the musician. It features previously unreleased live radio transcriptions originally broadcast in 1938.
after: The Joint Is Jumpin' is a compilation of recordings by pianist Fats Waller. It gathers Waller staples including "Ain't Misbehavin'," "Honeysuckle Rose," "Handful of Keys," "The Minor Drag," and the title number.
evidenceUsed: MB release (Fats Waller, secondaryType Compilation) + tracklist for track titles; year kept from ours, no hard date asserted

before: Jelly Roll Morton: The Complete Library of Congress Recordings is a 2005 box set of recordings from jazz pioneer Jelly Roll Morton. The set spans 128 tracks over eight CDs. It won two Grammy Awards in 2006, Best Historical Album and Best Album Notes.
after: The Library of Congress Recordings by Alan Lomax is a Jelly Roll Morton album issued on Rounder in 2006, drawn from Morton's Library of Congress recordings. It includes "King Porter Stomp," "Maple Leaf Rag," "I'm Alabama Bound," and "Mr. Jelly Lord."
evidenceUsed: MB release (Jelly Roll Morton, Rounder 11661-1897-2, 2006) + tracklist; 'Library of Congress recordings' from the MB release title itself, not the wrong-entity box-set wiki page

before: Don Ellis at Fillmore is a live double-LP by big band leader Don Ellis which was released in 1970. Later in his life he worked as a film composer, contributing a score to 1971's The French Connection and 1973's The Seven-Ups.
after: The New Don Ellis Band Goes Underground is a 1969 album by trumpeter and bandleader Don Ellis, released on Columbia. It leans on contemporary pop material and originals, with tracks such as "House in the Country," "Bulgarian Bulge," "Eli's Comin'," and "It's Your Thing."
evidenceUsed: MB release (The New Don Ellis Band, Columbia CS 9889, 1969) + tracklist for track titles

before: The Sermon is an album of spirituals by American jazz pianist Hampton Hawes recorded in 1958, but not released on the Contemporary label until 1987. A Contemporary Records session that documents pianist Hampton Hawes working through the cool jazz vocabulary of the hard bop period.
after: The Trio, Volume 3: Everybody Likes Hampton Hawes is a 1956 trio album by pianist Hampton Hawes on Contemporary Records. The program runs through standards including "Somebody Loves Me," "Embraceable You," "I Remember You," and "A Night in Tunisia," plus the original "The Sermon."
evidenceUsed: MB release (Hampton Hawes, Contemporary C3523, 1956) + tracklist for track titles
